5 min read
Just now

--

After working on FlightLog for a while, I recently returned to AeroType, my aircraft type dictionary project.
AeroType started as a simple web app for organizing aircraft type information. The goal was to create a clean aircraft dictionary that could later connect with my other aviation IT projects, especially FlightLog, my personal flight record manager.
In this update, I focused on making AeroType more detailed, more accurate, and more useful as an aircraft reference service.
The main improvements were:

• Adding aircraft photos to aircraft type cards

• Splitting large aircraft families into more precise categories

• Adding new aircraft manufacturers and aircraft types

• Improving aircraft filtering

• Preparing the data structure for future integration with FlightLog
Why I Updated AeroType
The first version of AeroType worked well as a simple aircraft type dictionary.
It allowed users to browse aircraft types, search by model, filter by manufacturer, and view detailed aircraft information.
However, as I continued building other aviation-related projects, I realized that AeroType needed to become more than a basic list of aircraft. If FlightLog is going to connect each personal flight record with aircraft type data, then AeroType needs to provide cleaner and more detailed aircraft information.
For example, an aircraft family such as the Boeing 737 is too broad to be represented as a single simple card. The 737 family has several major generations, and each generation has different technical, historical, and operational characteristics.
This update was mainly about improving that level of detail.
Adding Aircraft Photos
One of the most visible improvements was adding aircraft photos to the dictionary.
Each aircraft type card now feels more visual and easier to recognize. Instead of showing only text-based information, AeroType can now present aircraft types with real aircraft images.
This makes the dictionary feel more like an actual aircraft reference service.
It also improves the user experience because aircraft types are easier to distinguish visually, especially when comparing different families or generations.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Refining the Boeing 737 Family
One of the biggest data structure improvements was the Boeing 737 family.
Previously, the 737 information was grouped too broadly. Since the Boeing 737 has a long history and many variants, I decided to split it into four major generations:

• Boeing 737 Original

• Boeing 737 Classic

• Boeing 737 Next Generation

• Boeing 737 MAX
This makes the dictionary much easier to understand.
Each generation now has its own aircraft card and its own variant table. For example, the 737 Original focuses on the early 737–100 and 737–200, while the 737 MAX card focuses on the latest MAX 7, MAX 8, MAX 9, and MAX 10 variants.
This change made the data more organized and more historically accurate.
It also makes future integration with FlightLog easier. If a flight record uses a specific aircraft type, it can be linked to a more precise aircraft generation instead of a single broad 737 category.
Separating the Boeing 777 and 777X Families
I also separated the Boeing 777 family from the Boeing 777X family.
The original Boeing 777 family and the newer 777X family are closely related, but they represent different stages of development. The 777X includes major changes such as new wings, folding wingtips, and next-generation design improvements.
Because of that, I decided it made more sense to manage them as separate aircraft family cards.
This also allowed AeroType to show the 777X with a development status label, making the aircraft status clearer in the modal detail view.
This was a small change from a UI perspective, but it was an important improvement in terms of data modeling.
Adding McDonnell Douglas Aircraft
Another important update was adding McDonnell Douglas as a new manufacturer category.
Aviation history is not only about Airbus and Boeing. McDonnell Douglas played a major role in commercial aviation, and many of its aircraft are still well known among aviation enthusiasts.
For this update, I added two major McDonnell Douglas aircraft groups:

• MD-11 Family and MD-80 / MD-90 Series
The MD-11 is especially interesting because of its distinctive tri-jet configuration, with one engine mounted at the base of the vertical stabilizer.
The MD-80 and MD-90 series are also iconic aircraft, often recognized by their rear-mounted engines and T-tail design.
I also added a McDonnell Douglas filter button to the manufacturer filter menu, so users can browse only McDonnell Douglas aircraft with one click.
Improving the Dictionary Structure
This update reminded me that an aircraft dictionary is not just about adding more data.
The structure of the data matters.
If aircraft families are too broad, the information becomes unclear. If aircraft variants are mixed together without a clear hierarchy, users may have a hard time understanding the differences between models.
By separating aircraft families more carefully, AeroType became easier to browse and easier to expand.
The project now has a clearer structure for aircraft families, variants, manufacturers, production status, and detailed specifications.
This will be important when AeroType connects more deeply with FlightLog in the future.
Why This Matters for FlightLog Integration
AeroType and FlightLog are separate projects, but I am designing them to work together.
AeroType manages aircraft type information.
FlightLog manages personal flight records.
Eventually, when a user records a flight in FlightLog, the aircraft type should be able to connect to its corresponding AeroType page.
For example, a FlightLog record might include:
Aircraft Type: Boeing 787-10 DreamlinerWhen the user clicks that aircraft type, AeroType can open the correct aircraft dictionary page.
That kind of connection requires consistent aircraft type IDs, clear aircraft family separation, and reliable naming rules.
This AeroType update helps make that future integration more stable.
What I Learned
This update was a good reminder that improving a project is not always about adding completely new features.
Sometimes, improvement means making the existing data cleaner and more useful.
Through this update, I practiced:

• Data restructuring

• Aircraft family classification

• UI filtering improvements

• Local image integration

• Git commit organization

• Preparing data for future service integration
I also learned that domain knowledge matters when building data-based services.
For an aviation project, it is not enough to simply store aircraft names. Aircraft families, generations, variants, manufacturers, and production status all affect how the data should be organized.
This is where my interest in aviation and my study of Information Systems connect very naturally.
Next Steps
AeroType is becoming more detailed, but there is still more to improve.
My next plans include:

• Improving aircraft detail modals

• Expanding aircraft variant tables

• Improving the aircraft search experience

• Adding multilingual support in the future

• Connecting AeroType more deeply with FlightLog
Eventually, I want AeroType to become part of a broader aviation data platform, where aircraft type data, flight records, airline data, airport data, and route data can connect with each other.
Final Thoughts
This update made AeroType feel much more complete.
By adding aircraft photos, separating aircraft families more carefully, and expanding the manufacturer database, AeroType is becoming a stronger foundation for my aviation IT portfolio.
AeroType started as a simple aircraft type dictionary.
Now, it is slowly becoming a structured aviation reference system.
For me, this project is not only about building a web app. It is also about learning how to organize real-world domain data and turn it into a useful service.
As an Information Systems student with a strong interest in aviation, this is exactly the kind of project I want to keep developing.
